findings.title: Create Reddit accounts natively on mobile; never buy or karma-farm
findings.claim: Buying accounts or farming karma is the primary reason agencies get Reddit bans, so organic creation on mobile is safer.
findings.what_they_do: Accounts are created fresh on mobile. If web posting is needed, the account is transferred to an anti-detect browser using the same proxy it was created on. No purchased accounts, no karma farms — the account grows purely through the warmup period.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit traffic system (§5 GAP — traffic/distribution strategy not documented). This is a foundational building block we lack entirely.
findings.recommendation: Adopt this as the baseline Reddit account-creation rule: mobile-native creation, same proxy carried into any anti-detect browser, zero purchased/farmed accounts. Document it as Palm's Reddit SOP starting point.

sources.quote: we don't buy accounts or farm karma. Most people do this, and this is a big reason why people get banned

findings.title: 10–15 day organic Reddit warmup: scroll, 1–2 upvotes, 1 comment/day
findings.claim: A minimal 10–15 day warmup of only scrolling, light upvoting, and one comment per day is sufficient to avoid bans before posting begins.
findings.what_they_do: During warmup (10–15 days): scroll daily, upvote 1–2 posts, comment once per day, join a few SFW subreddits. No link in bio, no promotional activity. After warmup, posting starts at 1 post/day and ramps up by 1 post/day until hitting 20 posts/day. Link in bio is added only on the first posting day.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a mature 90-day warmup cadence for AI accounts (§4) but no formalized warmup for Reddit traffic accounts. Reddit warmup is far shorter and lighter than IG persona warmup.
findings.recommendation: Build a Reddit-specific warmup SOP: 10–15 days, activity caps as described, no link until Day 1 of posting. Add this as a parallel track to the existing AI IG warmup system, tracked in Airtable.

sources.quote: we warm up the account for 10 to 15 days. In the warm-up, all we do is just scroll every day, upvote one to two times, comment like once per day

findings.title: Use unique link-in-bio URLs per Reddit account; never reuse or direct-link OF
findings.claim: Reusing the same tracking link or OF link across multiple Reddit accounts is now a detectable ban trigger — every account must have a distinct intermediary link.
findings.what_they_do: Each Reddit account gets its own unique link using a link-in-bio tool (e.g., Linktree, Linkboo). No direct OnlyFans URLs. A pinned post promoting the OF link is added once the account reaches 20 posts/day — giving profile visitors a second discovery point beyond the bio.
findings.vs_us: No internal Reddit link-management system exists (§5 GAP). Palm uses Beacons for AI persona link-in-bio (§3) but there's no policy for Reddit-specific unique link rotation.
findings.recommendation: Standardize: one unique Linktree/Linkboo/Beacons URL per Reddit account, generated at account creation and logged in Airtable. Add a pinned-post step to the posting ramp-up SOP at the 20-posts/day milestone.

sources.quote: the link now needs to be different on every account and no straight OF link — use something like link boo

findings.title: Spoof every reused image and video before reposting across Reddit accounts
findings.claim: Posting the same unmodified image or video across multiple accounts is a ban signal; spoofing (metadata/fingerprint stripping) makes each upload appear unique to Reddit.
findings.what_they_do: Images are spoofed via a Telegram bot (e.g., Cleansely bot). Videos are uploaded to RedGifs and a new link is generated for each account/post — the distinct RedGifs URL acts as the spoof. Every time the same content is reused across accounts it gets a fresh spoof pass.
findings.vs_us: No spoofing SOP documented internally (§5 GAP). Palm's content pipeline (§1) handles social assets but has no Reddit-repost fingerprint-stripping step.
findings.recommendation: Add a spoofing step to any Reddit content workflow: route images through the Cleansely Telegram bot, upload videos to RedGifs and generate a fresh link per account. This should be a required gate before any cross-account Reddit post.

sources.quote: spoofing images, you can use like a Telegram bot. Cleansely bot is good. And for videos you can just use RedGifs — make a new link for each video

findings.title: Run niche-specific Reddit accounts (one niche per account) to build targeted fanbases
findings.claim: Niche-specific accounts outperform general accounts because a visitor who sees content matching what drew them in is more likely to subscribe or purchase.
findings.what_they_do: Each Reddit account is dedicated to a single content niche (e.g., ass, feet, boobs). All posts on that account stay within that niche. This creates a coherent profile that converts profile visitors into subscribers or custom-content buyers rather than bouncing them with off-niche content.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit niche-account strategy (§5 GAP). Palm's content pipeline (§1) is inspo-driven but not structured around per-account Reddit niching.
findings.recommendation: When building out Reddit traffic for a creator, map their content to 2–3 distinct niches and run a separate account per niche rather than one catch-all account. Test which niche converts best per creator before scaling accounts.

sources.quote: every account we run is niche specific. So for example one account can be ass one can be feet one can be boobs

findings.topic: Reddit content and caption testing cadence
findings.title: Test new content, captions, and subreddits on a biweekly cadence to avoid performance plateaus
findings.claim: Agencies that stop getting banned but still see no results fail because they never systematically test content, captions, and subreddit selection — biweekly iteration is the fix.
findings.what_they_do: Every ~two weeks: (1) review and refresh content — research top-performing posts in target subreddits and recreate similar content for the model; (2) test new caption styles — particularly engagement-bait formats (questions/statements that invite replies, e.g. 'I heard people here like big boobs'); (3) audit and swap subreddits — avoid overly saturated ones, trial new niches if current ones underperform. Content quality is ranked as the highest-leverage variable, ahead of subreddit choice and captions.
findings.vs_us: No Reddit testing cadence documented at Palm (§5 GAP). Palm's inspo engine (§1) does biweekly-style viral scoring for IG content — the same iterative logic should apply to Reddit.
findings.recommendation: Build a biweekly Reddit review ritual into the agency ops calendar: content refresh (research top posts in target subs, recreate for creator), caption A/B test, subreddit audit. Mirror the structure of Palm's existing IG inspo review cycle (§1) and log results in Airtable.

sources.quote: every other week you should be testing new things — content, captions, and subreddits
the right caption can make a post go from 100 upvotes to 1,000 upvotes

findings.title: Agency undercutting has normalized 20% commission — unsustainable floor
findings.claim: Agencies competing solely on price have driven commissions down to ~20%, which is too low to fund any genuine value-add for creators.
findings.what_they_do: TDM (CDM) identifies that the market has self-destructed through undercutting: agencies charge ~20% because they can't articulate differentiated value, which starves them of budget to actually deliver. TDM's counter-position is charging up to 50% by delivering a full-service business (content ideation, videography, editing, scheduling, multi-platform, collabs) — chatting is not even the lead value prop.
findings.vs_us: Palm's commission model is set per-creator at onboarding (§7) but there is no documented rationale, tier structure, or floor/ceiling. The split logic is a known #1 gap (§7 🔲). If Palm is competing at low commission rates without articulating a differentiated full-service offer, it risks the same race-to-bottom trap described here.
findings.recommendation: Document Palm's commission rationale explicitly: what services justify what rate, and what is the floor below which Palm declines a deal. Model TDM's framing — price against a full-service business delivery (content engine, scheduling, multi-platform), not against chatting alone. This gives sales language for acquisition conversations (§6) and justifies higher splits.

sources.quote: agencies have started undercutting one another just to get the business because they can't think of any other way to add value
it is now normal to charge 20% for your services and as a result not able to fund any actual value add to the creator

findings.topic: Full-service agency proposition vs. glorified chatting service
findings.title: Reframe agency value prop around traffic + content — not chatting
findings.claim: In 2025, the only defensible agency value-add is delivering more subscribers (traffic + brand/niche), not chatting, which is now a commodity.
findings.what_they_do: TDM explicitly lists what justifies a 50% commission: content creation, ideation, videography, scene-setting, outfit picking, editing, uploading, multi-device/multi-platform management, and collaborations. Chatting is deliberately excluded from this list as table stakes. The agency positions itself as 'we bring them a business,' not 'we add chatters.'
findings.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is genuinely differentiated — data-driven, inspo-scraping, 8-step pipeline — which directly maps to TDM's 'ideation + content creation' value layer. Palm also does multi-platform scheduling (§5) and AI persona scaling (§3). However, Palm's public/sales articulation of this proposition is not documented (§6 🔲 acquisition gap). Palm risks underselling itself in creator conversations.
findings.recommendation: Build a one-page agency value proposition document that leads with Palm's content engine (§1) and AI persona capability (§3) — not chatting. Use this in acquisition DMs and onboarding. Mirror TDM's framing: 'we bring you a business.' Chatting remains outsourced (§8) so do not lead with it; lead with traffic and content differentiation.

sources.quote: in 2025, the only way that you can add value to a creator is more subscribers and that's the harsh truth

we bring them content creation, ideation, videography, scene setting, outfit picking, the editing process, the uploading process, multiple devices across multiple accounts on multiple platforms, collaborations

findings.topic: Creator trust erosion — overcoming skepticism from bad agency experiences
findings.title: Creators have 3+ years of bad-agency trauma — acquisition pitch must address this directly
findings.claim: Creators have been repeatedly burned by immature agencies since 2022, making them far harder to sign and far less tolerant of underperformance.
findings.what_they_do: TDM acknowledges this as a structural market problem rather than a personal objection to handle. Their implied solution is to differentiate through verifiable, full-service delivery (the list above) so the offer is self-evidently different from the scammy agencies the creator has encountered. The pitch implicitly must pre-empt skepticism by showing substance, not just promises.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented objection-handling framework (§6 🔲). There is no recorded playbook for how to address creator skepticism or prior bad experiences. This is a known gap and TDM's framing gives vocabulary for it.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'creator trust' section to the acquisition SOP (when built). Script responses to common objections: 'I've been burned before,' 'I already have chatters,' 'What do you actually do?' Lead with proof points (content engine, past creator results, specific deliverables list) rather than generic promises. Consider a short 'here's what we do differently' loom/video as a leave-behind in outreach DMs.

sources.quote: they've now had three years of being scammed by immature agencies that have tried to make a quick buck overnight
creators that are less tolerant because they've had more bad experiences than they had previously

findings.title: Chatting alone is no longer a defensible agency service — treat it as hygiene, not differentiator
findings.claim: Chatting is now a low-leverage commodity because every creator either has their own team or can hire a professional chatting service cheaply.
findings.what_they_do: TDM explicitly deprioritizes chatting in their pitch and service stack — it is not mentioned in their list of value-adds. They argue agencies that lead with chatting are 'glorified chatting services' with no pricing power or retention leverage over creators. The strategic insight is that chatting is necessary but not sufficient, and should not be the primary revenue or value justification.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting (§8) and has roadmap items for in-house and AI chatting. This finding is directly relevant as build-toward intelligence: when Palm brings chatting in-house or via AI, it should be positioned as a cost/quality improvement, not as the primary value prop to creators. Palm's differentiation should remain the content engine (§1) and traffic capabilities.
findings.recommendation: As Palm builds toward in-house and AI chatting (§8 roadmap), frame it internally as a margin improvement and quality-control move — not a new creator pitch point. Externally, keep the creator value proposition anchored to content, growth, and multi-platform presence. Chatting is infrastructure, not the headline offer. This aligns with TDM's positioning and avoids the commodity trap.

sources.quote: lots of agencies will still provide a service that's just a glorified chatting service. You're not an agency, you're a chatting service, and chatting is low leverage
I didn't even mention chatting there because it's not important

findings.topic: Regulatory and compliance pressure — OF restrictions, banking, GDPR
findings.title: Compliance (GDPR, OF T&C, banking) is now a real operational cost — agencies ignoring it will be swallowed
findings.claim: Regulatory pressure from OnlyFans tightening restrictions, banking difficulties, and GDPR/ICO enforcement is now a genuine existential risk for agencies not investing in compliance.
findings.what_they_do: TDM explicitly names compliance with ICO (UK Information Commissioner's Office), GDPR, OnlyFans platform rules, and social media platform legalities as an operational requirement — not optional. They frame agencies that ignore this as naive and destined to fail. No specific implementation is described, but the call-to-action is to 'spend time working out how to comply.'
findings.vs_us: Palm has EU AI Act compliance built into its AI persona pipeline (§3 — AI label + watermark) but there is no documented GDPR, ICO, or OF compliance framework for the real-creator side of the business (§0/§11). This is a 🔲 GAP with no internal baseline. As Palm scales real creators, this gap becomes material.
findings.recommendation: Initiate a compliance audit for the real-creator operation: (1) data handling and storage for creator PII and fan data under GDPR/applicable law, (2) OnlyFans agency T&C adherence, (3) banking/payout structure. Assign ownership (likely Evan given tech/ops role). Document findings and create a living compliance checklist. This is not urgent-today but is a scaling prerequisite — TDM signals it is now table stakes for credible agencies.

sources.quote: Only Fans are tightening up their restrictions. The banking process is an absolute nightmare. GDPR is clamping down. Compliance is becoming a part of this business model.
spending time working out how to comply with the ICO or your corresponding governing body and your jurisdiction and the relevant parties around Only Fans and the legalities there

findings.topic: Traffic/subscriber growth as the #1 agency differentiator in 2025
findings.title: Traffic generation (not chatting) is the only non-commodity value-add agencies can offer in 2025
findings.claim: The singular competitive advantage for OFM agencies in 2025 is the ability to drive new subscribers — all other services are commoditized.
findings.what_they_do: TDM states explicitly that 'to add value as an agency to OnlyFans creators, you have to be good with traffic' and directs viewers to a dedicated marketing/traffic video as the core playbook. Traffic is positioned as the primary skill gap across the industry and the main reason agencies fail to differentiate. Specific traffic methods are not detailed in this video but are referenced as existing TDM content.
findings.vs_us: Traffic and distribution strategy is a documented 🔲 GAP at Palm (§5 — Reddit, paid, mass-DM, S4S not documented). Palm's content engine (§1) produces top-of-funnel social content which indirectly drives traffic, but there is no explicit traffic strategy codified. This finding strongly reinforces the §5 gap as the highest-priority thing to close.
findings.recommendation: Prioritize documenting and building Palm's traffic playbook as the next major systems initiative after the SMM hub redesign (§11). Source TDM's referenced traffic video and other competitor traffic content for tactics. Define Palm's traffic channels per creator (IG organic, Reddit, TikTok, paid, S4S, collabs) and assign ownership. This is the capability that justifies premium commission rates and creator retention — TDM is explicit that it is now the #1 differentiator.

sources.quote: in 2025 to add value as an agency to OnlyFans creators, you have to be good with traffic
in 2025, the only way that you can add value to a creator is more subscribers and that's the harsh truth and the vast majority is none of you know how to do that

findings.topic: Brand and niche as the content strategy foundation for creator differentiation
findings.title: Creators need distinct brand identity (not just niche) to cut through saturated OF market
findings.claim: The combination of a defined niche (market subsection) and a distinct brand (who the creator is) is what drives subscriber growth and justifies premium agency pricing.
findings.what_they_do: TDM distinguishes between niche (the category/subsection of the market a creator occupies) and brand (the creator's unique identity within that niche), using Alex Hormozi as an analogy — people recognize him by brand, not just by niche. TDM positions brand+niche strategy as the core of what they sell to creators and the reason they can charge 50%. This is content and positioning strategy rolled together.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is data-driven and inspo-led — it optimizes for viral replication of what works. This is strong for content volume and format, but does not explicitly build a creator's distinct brand identity or niche positioning. There is no documented brand/niche strategy layer in §1 or elsewhere. This is a meaningful gap: Palm can tell a creator what format to film, but may not be helping them define *who they are* in the market.
findings.recommendation: Add a brand/niche strategy step to creator onboarding (§6) and the content pipeline (§1). For each creator: (1) define their niche explicitly, (2) document their brand identity (visual style, personality, tone, content themes). Use this as the filter on inspo promotion — not just 'this reel is viral' but 'this reel fits this creator's brand.' This bridges Palm's content production strength with a positioning layer that justifies premium rates and improves creator retention.

sources.quote: The buzzwords on this channel are brand and niche
Niche is your subsection of the market. Branding is who you are.

findings.topic: Course-to-agency pipeline / education as lead gen
findings.title: Use coaching/education funnel as disguised agency creator acquisition
findings.claim: A coaching program sold to aspiring creators is effectively a pre-qualified pipeline of motivated models who will eventually want an agency to 'just do it for them' for a percentage.
findings.what_they_do: Camila's team sells a $2K–$8K coaching program to aspiring OF creators; the struggling graduates who want hands-on help become warm leads for the management agency itself. The education product pre-qualifies, pre-motivates, and pre-sells the creator on the agency's value before any outreach happens.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is entirely Josh-DM-led outbound with no inbound or content-driven funnel (§6). Palm has no educational product, community, or content vehicle that could self-select motivated creators and route them into the agency pipeline. This is a structurally different acquisition motion.
findings.recommendation: Consider building a lightweight inbound acquisition layer — e.g., a free YouTube/TikTok educational series or a low-cost mini-course ($47–$97) targeted at aspiring creators — where the CTA at the end is 'want us to do this for you?' This turns content into a self-qualifying creator pipeline without the reputational risk of a high-ticket program. Even a free community (like the speaker's Club 17) achieves the same warm-lead effect at near-zero cost.

sources.quote: every single one of these female followers is essentially warm lead for the coaching product but more for the agency
this whole thing is basically agency lead generation disguised as education

findings.title: Manufacture a high-emotion narrative event to generate mass inbound creator leads
findings.claim: A 'retirement documentary' narrative is a content marketing vehicle that drives massive organic traffic and surfaces tens of thousands of pre-qualified aspiring creators in 24 hours.
findings.what_they_do: The team produced a cinematic 50-minute YouTube documentary framed around personal transformation and retirement, generating 50,000 coaching applications within 24 hours — all organic, zero paid acquisition. The emotional narrative (growth, meaning, moving on) was engineered to resonate with aspiring creators who identify with the creator's journey.
findings.vs_us: Palm does zero content marketing for creator acquisition; all acquisition is manual outbound DMs (§6). Palm has no documented brand presence or narrative that would attract inbound creator applications.
findings.recommendation: While Palm cannot replicate a Mr. Beast-level personal brand, the underlying mechanic is replicable at smaller scale: a founder-facing YouTube or TikTok series documenting real creator success stories ('how we took a creator from $0 to $X') can generate inbound inquiries from aspiring creators who self-identify. Even one viral case-study video functions as a perpetual inbound acquisition asset.

sources.quote: the whole retirement narrative was just purely for marketing. Documentary was content to drive traffic. The course is lead generation

findings.title: Stack a high-ticket main offer ($2K–$8K) with a $67 downsell to capture all price points
findings.claim: Offering a downsell at a dramatically lower price point ($67) captures leads who can't afford the main program and keeps them in the ecosystem for future upselling.
findings.what_they_do: Coaching tiers run $2,000–$8,000 for the core program; prospects who decline are redirected to a $67 mini-course. This two-tier structure maximizes revenue per lead by capturing both high-intent and price-sensitive audiences while maintaining a relationship with the lower tier for eventual upsell.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing model for any client-facing offer beyond per-creator commission (§7 — flagged as the #1 gap). No offer stack, no downsell logic exists internally.
findings.recommendation: Directly applicable to Palm's future education/lead-gen layer if pursued (per finding above). Even within agency deal structures, Palm should document a tiered commission/service offer: e.g., a lighter 'launch package' at lower commission for unproven creators, stepping up to full management as revenue scales — giving Palm a 'yes' at multiple price points and reducing friction from creators who won't commit to full-management terms upfront.

sources.quote: tears going from like 2 grand up to 8 grand for the main program. And if you can't afford that, there's this little downsell like $67 mini course they redirect you to

findings.topic: Creator vs. agency as the real business
findings.title: Recognize the agency is the real business; the creator is just the brand/face
findings.claim: The creator is never the actual business — the creator is the face, and the agency operation behind them is the scalable, durable asset.
findings.what_they_do: The speaker explicitly frames Camila's entire public persona, earnings, and documentary as a marketing instrument for the underlying agency/management business. The business model insight is: creators have capped individual leverage, but agencies that manage many creators have uncapped scaling. The agency owns the system; the creator rents her face to it.
findings.vs_us: This is directionally aligned with how Palm is structured (§0 — dual stream, revenue split), but Palm hasn't fully codified or articulated this framing internally as a strategic principle. Palm's AI persona program (§3) is the clearest expression of 'agency owns the asset, not the creator' — worth reinforcing in how Palm pitches itself and thinks about creator relationships.
findings.recommendation: Palm should internalize and document this framing explicitly: the agency's value is in the system (content pipeline, social growth, DM monetization), not in any one creator. This protects Palm from leverage risk when real creators churn and strengthens the case for the AI persona line as a strategic hedge. Also useful in creator acquisition pitches — Palm brings the machine, creators bring the face.

sources.quote: the creator is almost never the actual business. The creator is the face. She's the brand essentially

findings.topic: Free community as agency brand/acquisition engine
findings.title: Run a free community (e.g. 'Club 17') to build agency brand, trust, and inbound pipeline
findings.claim: A free community of fellow agency operators builds authority, generates referrals, and creates a low-friction top-of-funnel that continuously surfaces both creator and operator leads.
findings.what_they_do: The speaker runs 'Club 17,' a free community of 13,000+ agency owners, as his primary brand and distribution vehicle. It serves as a trust-building platform, a content distribution channel, and presumably a feeder into paid offers or partnerships. The community itself is the acquisition engine — members recruit other members, and the speaker's authority compounds.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no community, no public-facing brand, and no content marketing presence (§6 — acquisition gap). All brand equity is internal. Palm is invisible to the market of aspiring creators and agency operators.
findings.recommendation: Palm doesn't need to build a 13,000-person community overnight, but a modest Discord or Skool community of creators (current and prospective) could serve as: (1) a warm pool of creator leads, (2) a retention signal for existing creators who feel community belonging, and (3) a trust-building vehicle for Palm's brand. Even a private 'Palm Creator Network' with 50 members is a structural asset today that Palm lacks entirely.

sources.quote: that's what me and 13,000 other agency owners actually teach inside Club 17. How to build and scale an agency

findings.topic: Creator lifecycle — from struggle to managed
findings.title: Target creators mid-struggle post-launch as highest-conversion acquisition moment
findings.claim: Creators who have tried and struggled independently are the highest-intent, lowest-resistance acquisition targets for an agency because they've self-qualified their desire and experienced the pain of doing it alone.
findings.what_they_do: The Camila/Owen model implicitly identifies this: the coaching program surfaces struggling graduates, who then convert to managed clients. The agency doesn't cold-pitch unaware girls — it waits for the ones who already tried, already spent money learning, and now want someone to execute for them. This is a fundamentally different acquisition timing strategy.
findings.vs_us: Palm's outbound DM acquisition (§6) presumably targets creators at any stage. There's no documented targeting of 'post-attempt' creators — women who tried OF independently, didn't get traction, and are now open to management. This cohort likely has much higher close rates and lower renegotiation risk than cold prospects.
findings.recommendation: Add a targeting layer to Palm's acquisition outreach: specifically identify and message creators with existing (but underperforming) OF pages — accounts that show posting history but low engagement/subscriber counts. This signals they already want to do it, already tried, and are likely frustrated. Tailor the pitch to 'you've done the hard part, let us handle the rest.' Expect higher conversion and lower negotiation friction versus cold targets.

sources.quote: Some of them are going to struggle. Some of them are going to want more help. And some of them are going to wish someone would just do it for them

findings.title: Click-progression principle: SFW on social, escalate to OF, never give away the endgame free
findings.claim: Every platform touchpoint (social → link-in-bio → OF) must be a progressive escalation in explicitness so fans always expect more behind the next paywall.
findings.what_they_do: Agency enforces a strict content ladder: social media is most SFW, link-in-bio slightly sexier, OF page SFW on the feed with nothing explicit shown publicly. If a creator posts full nudity on Reddit, fans arrive on OF already satisfied and won't buy PPV. OF feed stays fully SFW so the only way to see more is to engage in DMs. This 'straight-line' funnel concept (borrowed from Jordan Belfort's straight-line persuasion) governs every content and chatting decision.
findings.vs_us: Palm's §2 (OF vault/PPV) is THIN — no documented content-progression or SFW/NSFW gating strategy for the OF feed. Palm's §1 social pipeline is strong but doesn't yet encode rules about how explicit social content should be relative to OF content. This is a direct gap Palm's content + chatting roadmap should address.
findings.recommendation: Codify a content-tier policy per creator: define max explicitness allowed on each platform (IG/TikTok = SFW, Reddit = tasteful/suggestive, OF public feed = SFW/lingerie, OF DMs/PPV = explicit). Bake this into creator onboarding (§6) and brief the external chat team so PPV sequencing starts from the right baseline.

sources.quote: the click progression basically every click they do needs to be a progression
if you post porn on Reddit you won't be able to sell mirror pictures or air because then it's a porn star
I don't like to show any nudes but it really depends on the girl and the niche

findings.title: A/B test two welcome message formats (video vs voice note) across cohorts and track reply rates
findings.claim: Welcome messages should be systematically A/B tested across fan cohorts to maximize reply rate, treating it as a data problem rather than a creative one.
findings.what_they_do: Agency runs two different welcome messages in alternating weekly cohorts (e.g. video week 1, voice note week 2, each across 100 new fans) and tracks reply counts. The better-performing format becomes the control; a third variant is then tested against it. Welcome message content: short video or voice note of creator being bubbly/joyful, ending with open-ended questions ('Why did you subscribe?', 'Where did you find me?', 'How can I improve my page?') — questions designed to extract traffic-source data and fan preference data simultaneously. No 'Hey baby' or price offers in the welcome message.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP for welcome messages. This is direct build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap. The data-collection angle (why did you sub, where from) also feeds back into Palm's content pipeline (§1) — if most fans cite a specific visual attribute, content ideation should reflect that.
findings.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, mandate a structured welcome-message A/B test protocol from day one: two formats, weekly cohorts of ≥50, track reply % and first-purchase %. Also add traffic-source and fan-preference questions to the welcome message so data flows back into the §1 content engine.

sources.quote: a video of the creator saying thank you for subscribing then it needs to be a 10 to 20 second video free of course looking cute bubbly joyous
experts do is they have 100 they will have two welcome messages that they will try over the week and they will track the numbers
don't use the word baby ask them where they're from what they like about the girl why did they subscribe

findings.title: Build GFE rapport via branching conversation + push-pull sexual teasing before any sell
findings.claim: Fans only spend freely when they are simultaneously in love and horny; rapport-building through natural, branching conversation that intermittently teases sexual content achieves both states before any PPV is offered.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to: (1) ask about name/age/location and immediately weave a personal story around each answer to create organic conversation flow rather than an interview; (2) gather 'trigger points' — pets, past relationships, job, childhood — and store them as notes to re-use later; (3) insert brief sexual teases (text-only) 2–3 times mid-conversation then snap back to normal chat, letting the fan steer toward sex on his own by the 4th–5th iteration; (4) use voice notes interleaved with text to add perceived authenticity ('10x rapport builder'). No selling until the fan is visibly pushing toward sexual content himself. Chatters are explicitly told to embody the girl's emotional state while typing.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline for rapport methodology (§8, fully outsourced). This is core build-toward material for the in-house chat team. Palm's whale-hunting system (§9) captures spending data but not the conversational trigger-point notes this approach depends on.
findings.recommendation: Draft a chatter SOP section on rapport-building that includes: (a) the push-pull sexual tease cadence, (b) mandatory trigger-point note-taking fields (pet names, job, relationship status, location, key traumas), (c) voice-note usage guidelines. Consider whether the Fan Tracker CRM (§9) can be extended to store per-fan trigger-point notes accessible to all chatters on shift.

sources.quote: you don't be like hey what's your name hey where you from no not this you have to ask good questions and you have to make sure there's a conversation flow
add a sexual tease with text and then come back to normal conversation and do it a couple times and he'll start getting horny
if you can add voice notes in between the teasing in between asking questions this will build rapport 10x

findings.topic: Dynamic PPV pricing ladder within a session
findings.title: Escalate PPV price with each media sent; calibrate based on fan's reply speed and excitement level
findings.claim: PPV pricing should not be fixed — chatters must dynamically increase price with each progressive media piece, reading fan excitement signals (reply speed, language, buy speed) to decide how aggressively to jump.
findings.what_they_do: Agency runs a content sequence from freebies → low-price PPV → escalating PPVs. First 2–3 items are free (selfie video, 1–2 photos — suggestive but not explicit). First paid item starts at $5–$15. If the fan buys immediately and shows strong appreciation, the next item jumps to $20–$25 or higher. Chatter has discretion to skip mid-tier content and offer the most exclusive item at a premium if the fan's excitement is peaking. Price is never negotiated downward — any attempt to bargain is reframed as disrespectful ('I'm not a prostitute'). No bundle pricing. After each purchase, chatter engages in conversation rather than immediately sending the next PPV, to reset excitement.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7, major gap). The external chat team (§8) may have its own approach but Palm has no visibility or control. This dynamic ladder methodology is the most concrete pricing framework found in competitor content and directly addresses Palm's §7 gap.
findings.recommendation: Document a PPV pricing framework for the in-house chat team roadmap: (1) freebie tier (3–4 items, SFW-to-suggestive), (2) entry PPV $10–15, (3) escalation rule (+$10–15 per step if fan buys fast), (4) no negotiation policy with reframe scripts. Also brief the current external chat team on the no-bundle, no-discount policy as a near-term improvement.

sources.quote: every time you move through the sequence you need to upgrade the price you need to upcharge every single thing you sell
if he's showing you a lot of appreciation and he's buying it right away you can upcharge the next one a little bit more
you cannot be negotiating like a man you cannot be like this picture $50 oh you don't want to do $50 I can do $45 no

findings.title: Post-session 'aftercare' — emotional conversation after purchase to prevent buyer's remorse and schedule next session
findings.claim: After a PPV sequence completes, chatters must transition to emotional/therapeutic conversation to cement attachment, prevent buyer's remorse, and book the next session before closing.
findings.what_they_do: After the sexual sequence ends: (1) give 1–2 freebies and say 'this is the best experience I've ever had, I've never done this before' to reinforce exclusivity and prevent regret; (2) engage in deep emotional conversation — childhood, relationships, job stress — acting as therapist to create emotional dependency; (3) before conversation dies, cut it intentionally and schedule next session ('are you free next weekend?'), let the fan name the date; (4) note the scheduled date in Notion with a reminder to message 2 days before; (5) never ignore a spending fan between sessions — give them extra attention and care. Fans who feel emotionally cared for post-purchase are far less likely to churn.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8 outsourced; §9 retention strategy is a GAP). This aftercare protocol directly addresses Palm's retention gap (§9) — it is the mechanism that converts a one-time buyer into a recurring whale. The Notion-based scheduling mentioned maps conceptually to what Palm could build in Fan Tracker CRM.
findings.recommendation: Add aftercare as a mandatory phase in the in-house chatter SOP: define it as a distinct conversation mode triggered post-purchase, include the therapist-conversation script structure, and require chatters to log the next scheduled session date in the Fan Tracker CRM (§9). Also explore a 'going-cold' alert that fires if a previously active fan hasn't been contacted within X days of their logged session date.

sources.quote: aftercare is very very important once you go through the whole sequence you want the fan to have overall positive remembrance and have no buyers remorse
after getting off guys get emotional because of the postnut clarity so you can talk deep stuff about him with him emotionally
I really like talking to you are you free next weekend let the man lead let him tell a date and you hit him up a couple days before

findings.topic: Fan CRM — emoji tagging + name encoding system
findings.title: Encode fan profile in OF username field using emojis + name bracket system for instant chatter context
findings.claim: Chatters working at scale cannot read notes for every fan before each message; an emoji-tag and name-encoding system embedded directly in the OF fan nickname gives instant context without leaving the chat window.
findings.what_they_do: Agency uses two overlapping systems: (1) Emoji tags prepended to fan nicknames — each emoji maps to a fan attribute (student, boyfriend-mode whale, PPV-only buyer, body-part preference, payday pending, session scheduled, time-waster, etc.); multiple emojis stack so a chatter sees the full profile at a glance. (2) Name encoding: OF allows editing fan display names, so 'JohnWick24' becomes '[student emoji][whale emoji] James (Batman) – 24 – Italy'. Name = real first name, bracket = nickname the chatter uses, dash = age, dash = location. Supplemented by a Notion CRM for full notes and a shift-handover summary system where outgoing chatters brief incoming chatters on key active fans.
findings.vs_us: Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9) captures spending data and going-cold signals but there is no documented emoji/name-encoding system for in-chat speed. Palm's chatting is currently outsourced (§8), but when building the in-house team this system is directly implementable at near-zero cost. It also maps well to Palm's tech stack (Airtable could store the emoji legend; the portal could surface it).
findings.recommendation: Define a standardized emoji taxonomy for fan classification (spending tier, archetype, preferred content, payday flag, session flag) and encode it into a chatter training SOP. Build a legend reference card in the admin portal or Airtable that any chatter can pull up. Extend the Fan Tracker (§9) to include a 'fan nickname string' field that chatters copy into OF.

sources.quote: I have a big list of emojis that we put next to the names so when the chatter is chatting he doesn't have to look through the list
John Wick 24 we put his name as James bracket whatever nickname you give him a dash his age dash where he's from and emojis in the start
the way to transfer the knowledge from one chatter to another is using these systems plus the fan notes plus we have a shift handover system

findings.title: Segment fans into typed lists (GFE, PPV-only, whale, time-waster) and send mass PPVs only to PPV-only list
findings.claim: Sending mass PPVs to all fans destroys perceived value for GFE fans and whales; PPV blasts should be sent only to the segment of fans who explicitly want transactional content.
findings.what_they_do: Agency maintains fan lists: (1) GFE/relationship fans — receive no mass PPVs, only personalized DM chatting sequences; (2) PPV-only fans — actively want to buy content, no GF interest, receive targeted PPV blasts with personalized captions; (3) whales — high-spenders who may or may not know the game, get maximum attention; (4) time-wasters — get minimal chatter time. List membership is set via OF native fan lists plus CRM. When sending a PPV blast, the agency pulls the PPV-only or body-part-specific list (e.g. 'ass fans') and sends with a caption written to feel personal. Student fans get lower-priced PPVs. The system prevents burning high-value GFE fans with transactional messages that break immersion.
findings.vs_us: Palm's whale-hunting module (§9) tracks spending tiers but there is no documented fan-type segmentation that governs what kind of message each fan receives. The external chat team (§8) likely has its own approach. This segmentation framework is directly actionable for the in-house chat roadmap and could be reflected in Fan Tracker CRM fields.
findings.recommendation: Add fan-type classification (GFE, PPV-buyer, whale, time-waster) as a required field in the Fan Tracker CRM (§9). Establish a policy: mass PPV blasts are only sent to the PPV-buyer segment. Create sub-segments by content preference for targeted sequences. Brief the external chat team on this segmentation to improve current revenue immediately.

sources.quote: you don't want to send PPVs to the fans that are regularly talking to the girl that feels a natural connection thinks of them as their boyfriend or are whales
you make an ass sequence for all the guys that like ass now all you do is you pull up the ass list you send everyone a PPV you put a caption in a way that it feels personalized
students okay that means put the price low he's not going to be paying that much because he's a student

findings.title: Use video + follow-up voice note as welcome message (not text/PPV blast)
findings.claim: A video welcome message followed by a voice note follow-up outperforms text or immediate PPV blasts because it gives the creator personality and realism, making fans more willing to spend.
findings.what_they_do: Send a pre-recorded video as the welcome message, then follow up with a voice note hours later or the next day if no reply. The combo lets the fan 'imagine the girl talking to her phone,' adding realism. No baby/generic openers — message tone is bubbly, personal, and non-transactional.
findings.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is currently outsourced to an external chat team (§8). No internal SOP exists for welcome message format or sequencing. This is direct build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap.
findings.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, establish a standard welcome sequence: (1) creator-recorded video message at subscribe, (2) voice note follow-up sent next day if no reply. Document this as the default template and A/B test variants per the A/B finding below.

sources.quote: A voice note and B videos. These are the two best methods in my opinion.
if you have a video, the best way is to do a video and then a followup voice message. It gives a lot of personality to the girl.

findings.title: Open with curiosity questions (location, feedback, how they found page) not generic 'hey baby'
findings.claim: Asking unique, genuine questions — where are you from, how did you find the page, how can I improve — gets fans talking because no other agency does this, whereas generic openers ('hey baby', 'what are you looking for') read as transactional and get ignored.
findings.what_they_do: Welcome message asks: where are you from, what do you like about the creator, how did you find the page, and how can the creator improve her OF. The 'improvement' ask is positioned as a disarming authenticity move — fans who answer reveal exactly what they want, giving chatters intel for future monetization. Tone is bubbly and positive throughout. Explicitly avoid the word 'baby' in early messages.
findings.vs_us: No internal welcome message script or opener SOP exists at Palm (§8 — outsourced). This is a concrete scripting framework to feed into the in-house chat team build and/or briefing the existing external team.
findings.recommendation: Draft a welcome message script template using these four curiosity questions. Brief the current external chat team on this approach now as a quick win. Codify as the baseline SOP when building in-house. Add a field in Fan Tracker CRM to log the traffic source fans self-report (maps to §1 inspo/traffic data).

sources.quote: Ask them where they're from, what they like about the girl, why did they subscribe or where did they find the profile from? How can you improve the page?
In 2025, man, we don't use baby, you know, and give it out for free. It needs to be authentic.

findings.title: Position creator as genuine connection, not a content store — avoid immediate PPV blasts
findings.claim: Fans who receive only PPV blast welcome messages treat the creator like Amazon (transactional, no loyalty), find no reason to return, and churn; fans who feel genuine connection become repeat buyers and whales.
findings.what_they_do: Explicitly prohibit immediate PPV bundle blasts or 'buy $10 PPV' openers in welcome messages. Chatters are trained to embody the creator's emotions while typing and to think like the girl, making responses feel authentic. The frame set in message one is 'real girl, not an agency, not just selling content.'
findings.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no documented anti-blast policy or GFE framing guideline. This is foundational philosophy for the in-house chat SOP — especially relevant because Palm's retention data (§9) likely shows churn correlating with low-quality early DM experiences.
findings.recommendation: Make 'no PPV blast in welcome message' an explicit rule in any chat team brief (internal or external). Add GFE framing guidelines to the future in-house chatter onboarding SOP. Consider auditing what the current external team's welcome message actually looks like against this standard.

sources.quote: when the fan finds no connection, the fan is like, okay, this is just a store, it's a website, Amazon, I go, I click, I buy.
what they need is a genuine connection from a real girl, a reason to come back, someone to be their therapist.

findings.title: A/B test two welcome message variants across weekly fan cohorts and track reply rates
findings.claim: Systematically testing two different welcome message formats (e.g., video vs. voice note) across consecutive weekly cohorts of ~100 new fans and comparing reply rates can lift engaged conversations from ~3 to ~20–30 out of 100, multiplying pipeline for whales.
findings.what_they_do: Run welcome message variant A for week-one new subscribers (~100 fans), variant B for week-two new subscribers (~100 fans). Track reply count for each cohort. Pick the winner or iterate a third variant. Frame it as a numbers game: 3 replies → $10; 20–30 replies → multiple whale candidates. Data drives script decisions, not gut feel.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a strong data-driven testing loop for social content (§1 — viral score, inspo pipeline) but no equivalent testing infrastructure for DM/chat performance. The Fan Tracker / whale-hunting system (§9) captures fan data but not welcome message conversion metrics.
findings.recommendation: Add a welcome-message variant tracking layer to the Fan Tracker or a dedicated Airtable view: log which message variant a fan received, date, and whether they replied within 24/48 hrs. Run weekly cohort A/B cycles once the in-house team is live. Share this methodology with the current external chat team as an interim improvement.

sources.quote: they will have two welcome messages that they will try over the week and they'll track the numbers across 100 people.
now you have 20 people that you're talking to that are potential buyers, potential relationship, potential whales.

findings.title: Send timed follow-up (voice note or PPV photo) to non-repliers hours/next-day after welcome
findings.claim: Most new fans won't reply to the initial welcome message but will engage on the first follow-up, so chatters must be trained to send a second touch (voice note, text, or soft PPV photo) within a few hours or the next day.
findings.what_they_do: If a fan doesn't reply to the welcome message, chatters wait a few hours to one day and send a follow-up — either a voice note, a conversational text, or a PPV photo with a soft 'we haven't talked yet, let's get to know each other' hook. Stories are also cited as a parallel re-engagement tool to pull silent fans back into DMs.
findings.vs_us: No internal follow-up sequencing SOP exists at Palm (§8 — outsourced). Unknown whether the external chat team runs structured follow-ups or lets non-repliers go cold. This is a gap in the current outsourced arrangement and a key SOP element for the in-house build.
findings.recommendation: Define a two-step DM sequence as the minimum standard: Step 1 = welcome message (video/voice note), Step 2 = follow-up within 24 hrs for non-repliers (voice note or soft PPV). Include timing guidelines in any brief to the external team now. Build this into the in-house chatter training SOP.

sources.quote: Most of the fans you will get on the first followup, which should be a voice note or a text message that you need to train your chatters to give at least a few hours or the next day.
stories is a very good tool to actually get more people in the DM.

findings.title: Use welcome message to capture traffic source per fan and feed data back into content/marketing
findings.claim: Asking fans where they found the creator in the welcome message generates a data loop: over time you learn which traffic source brings which fan type and can sculpt marketing content and chat scripts to match what those fans want.
findings.what_they_do: Welcome message explicitly asks 'where did you find my profile?' Chatters log or pattern-match answers over time to identify which platform (Reddit, IG, TikTok, etc.) brings fans who respond to specific content types (e.g., fans from a certain source like the creator's eyes). That insight feeds back into content production decisions and PPV script design.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a sophisticated inspo-driven content pipeline (§1) and partial fan intelligence (§9 — Fan Tracker, whale hunting) but no documented loop connecting fan-reported traffic source to content or script adjustments. This closes a gap between §1 (content) and §9 (fan data) using DM intel as the bridge.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'reported traffic source' field to the Fan Tracker CRM. Train chatters (external now, in-house later) to log this from welcome message replies. Feed aggregated source data into the content pipeline review step (§1 Step 4 — inspo review) to inform what content angles to prioritize per platform.

sources.quote: where did he find the profile from. And over time you will kind of find out which traffic source brings what kind of fans, what do they like.
using that data you can sculpt your marketing content and you can sculpt your scripts.
